coredata.dc:title: Construction of microspherical flower-like Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr S-scheme heterojunction for photocatalytic elimination of nitrofurazone and Cr (VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jiangli
Yang, Jinhang
Liang, Jiaxiang
Tu, Lingli
Bin, Yuejing
Hou, Yanping
coredata.dc:description: It is crucial to design efficient photocatalysts for elimination of combined pollutants in wastewater. Herein, the S-scheme Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr (ZBA) heterojunction was synthesized through co-modification of Zn3In2S6 with B-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) and AgBr nanoparticles using solvothermal method for synchronous removals of nitrofurazone (NFZ) and Cr (VI). Characterization results revealed that ZBA showed superior photocatalytic activity than the counterparts. In NFZ-Cr (VI) coexistence environment, removal efficiencies towards NFZ and Cr (VI) were 95.23% and 99.39% using the ZBA photocatalyst within 2 h of visible light irradiation. Results demonstrated that ∙OH, h+ and ∙O2 − played major role for NFZ removal, while mainly e- and ∙O2 − contributed to Cr (VI) reduction. The S-scheme heterojunction mechanism of ZBA was confirmed by energy band structure analysis, ESR and calculated work function.Possible pathways and mechanisms of NFZ degradation were proposed according the identified intermediates. Besides, the ecotoxicity of intermediates was analyzed. This study advances the development of photocatalytic treatment of combined-polluted wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S-scheme heterojunction
Cr (VI)
Nitrofurazone
Reactive species
Degradation pathways

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Operations for photocatalytic removals of pollutants and analytical methods
Effect of various factors on photocatalytic performance
The stability of the ZAB in NFZ-Cr (VI) coexistence environment
Degradation pathways of NFZ and toxicity analysis of the intermediates

Foundation of Research Project of Bosch Branch of Guangxi University Graduate School
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21707021 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2017GXNSFBA198186, 2020GXNSFAA297034), Foundation of Research Project of Bosch Branch of Guangxi University Graduate School (GXU-BFY-2020-036), and Central Government Funds for Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development (2021ZYZX2089).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of AgBr (a, b), Zn3In2S6 (c, d), ZB (e, f), ZBA (g, h); TEM images of ZAB (i,j); HRTEM of ZBA (k).
XRD patterns of AgBr, Zn3In2S6, ZB, ZBA samples.
(a) XPS spectra of ZBA sample, (b) high resolution spectrum of Ag 3d, (c) Br 3d, (d) Zn 2p, (e) In 3d, (f) S 2p, (g) B 1 s, (h) C1s and (i) O1s.
The UV–vis DRS (a) and Eg plots (b) of AgBr, Zn3In2S6, ZB, ZBA. Mott-Schottky plots of AgBr (c) and ZB (d) at different frequencies.
(a) PL spectra, (b) TRPL spectra, (c) transient photocurrent and (d) EIS nyquist plots of samples.

In the NFZ-Cr (VI) coexistence system, the photocatalytic removing curve of NFZ (a) and Cr (VI) (b) by samples; the pseudo-first order reaction kinetics for the NFZ (c) and Cr (VI) (d) removing by samples. (Conditions: 0.5 g/L samples; NFZ/Cr (VI) = 10 mg/L, 100 mL; pH = 7).

Photooxidation of NFZ and photoreduction of Cr (VI) by ZBA with different situations (a); photocatalytic activity of ZBA photocatalyst in the NFZ-Cr (VI) coexisting system: effect of initial NFZ-Cr (VI) concentration (b) and effect of ZBA photocatalyst dosage (c-d).
Photocatalytic activity of ZBA photocatalyst: (a-b) effect of initial solution pH; (c-d) effect of water source; (e-f) effect of inorganic anions.

(a) Cyclic tests of NFZ degradation and Cr (VI) reduction by ZBA; (b) XRD patterns of ZBA before and after photocatalytic cycling for five times. (Conditions: 0.5 g/L ZBA; NFZ/Cr (VI) = 10 mg/L, 100 mL; pH = 7).
(a) Effect of various radical scavengers on the photocatalytic removal of NFZ and Cr (VI) by samples. (b) ESR spectra of DMPO-∙O2 − and DMPO-∙OH over samples under visible light.
(a) The calculated work function of the (111) plane of AgBr and (b) the calculated work function of the (102) plane of Zn3In2S6; (c-e) schematic illustration of S-scheme charges transfer process.
(a) XPS spectra and (b) high-resolution XPS spectra of ZBA (after four cycles of photocatalytic use).
The degradation pathway of NFZ in simultaneous photocatalytic redox conversion of NFZ and Cr (VI) system by ZBA.
Predicted acute and chronic toxicity of NFZ and its degradation intermediates using ECOSAR system.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism of ZBA in NFZ and Cr (VI) coexistence environment under visible light.

head.ce:title: Construction of microspherical flower-like Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr S-scheme heterojunction for photocatalytic elimination of nitrofurazone and Cr (VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Light Industry and Food Engineering, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Resource Engineering
Wuzhou Engineering Research Center of Resource Recycling

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical and Resource Engineering, Wuzhou University, Wuzhou Engineering Research Center of Resource Recycling, Wuzhou 543002, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is crucial to design efficient photocatalysts for elimination of combined pollutants in wastewater. Herein, the S-scheme Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr (ZBA) heterojunction was synthesized through co-modification of Zn3In2S6 with B-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) and AgBr nanoparticles using solvothermal method for synchronous removals of nitrofurazone (NFZ) and Cr (VI). Characterization results revealed that ZBA showed superior photocatalytic activity than the counterparts. In NFZ-Cr (VI) coexistence environment, removal efficiencies towards NFZ and Cr (VI) were 95.23% and 99.39% using the ZBA photocatalyst within 2 h of visible light irradiation. Results demonstrated that ∙OH, h+ and ∙O2 − played major role for NFZ removal, while mainly e- and ∙O2 − contributed to Cr (VI) reduction. The S-scheme heterojunction mechanism of ZBA was confirmed by energy band structure analysis, ESR and calculated work function.Possible pathways and mechanisms of NFZ degradation were proposed according the identified intermediates. Besides, the ecotoxicity of intermediates was analyzed. This study advances the development of photocatalytic treatment of combined-polluted wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to low removal efficiency of traditional wastewater treatment technologies and improper management of aquaculture wastewater discharge, large amounts of wastewaters containing antibiotics and heavy metals are discharged into water environment, endangering ecosystems and human beings [1,2]. Nitrofurazone (NFZ), a synthetic nitrofurans antibiotic, has been widely used in aquaculture for infectious treatment, causing great menace to ecological environment [3]. Heavy metals like chromium, copper and zinc have been widely used in animal infectious disease prevention and animal feed production [4]. Therefore, antibiotics and heavy metals could be co-existed in wastewaters, such as tetracycline-Cu, tetracycline-Cr(VI), etc. Generally, heavy metal chromium usually exists in the ecosystem in different oxidation states [5–7], while Cr (VI) is a highly toxic carcinogenic and mutagenic agent [8,9]. For the potential threat of NFZ and Cr (VI), it is an urgent task to explore effective technologies to treat combined pollution with antibiotics and heavy metals.

Photocatalytic process has been considered to be a promising plan for efficient removal of complex pollutants, since it holds the advantages of using sunlight as the sustainable driving force, high efficiency, and with mild reaction conditions [10–12]. In this regard, design and preparation of photocatalytic materials with efficient carriers′ separation and strong redox potential should be highly desired for coinstantaneous removal of antibiotics and heavy metals[13,14].

Recently, Zn3In2S6 photocatalyst has attracted vast attention caused by the applicable band gap (∼2.8 eV) and its excellent photochemical properties [15,16]. Zn3In2S6-based semiconductors have been developed for pollutants removals [17]. For example, Yin et al. prepared Ti3C2 MXene/Zn3In2S6 for Cr (VI) removal, and the the highest removal from the optimized sample was 3.17 times of Zn3In2S6 [18]. Zhuang et al. reported that the Zn3In2S6/Bi2MoO6 composite showed satisfactory tetracycline removal performance [19]. However, application of Zn3In2S6-based photocatalysts for simultaneous removals of complex pollutants is still limited. Recently, our team synthesized Zn3In2S6/AgBr heterojunction for synchronous elimination of metronidazole and Cr (VI), and optimum removal efficiencies of 98.2% and 94.8% were achieved within 120 min. However, there was still room for further optimization of Zn3In2S6-based photocatalyst, and their photocatalytic activity towards other combined pollutants should be estimated. Moreover, deeper insights into the photocatalytic mechanism should be revealed. Besides, the toxicity of degradation intermediates during photocatalytic degradation process is also one of the concerns.

Quasi-0D carbon materials, like graphene quantum dots (GQDs), usually have abundant active sites and could facilitate fast electron transfer [20,21]. Hence, it is believed that using GQDs to modify Zn3In2S6 could achieve better performance. Meanwhile, the introduction of N, S, B and other heteroatoms into nanocarbon materials can adjust the inherent electrical and chemical properties of nanocarbon materials by changing the electronic structure [22]. Our group previously implanted the B-doped GQDs into bimetallic organic framework through the M−O−C bonding interface by a simple electrodeposition method, endowing the material efficient charge transfer ability and abundant active sites [23]. Therefore, as Zn3In2S6 nanosphere structure can provide large specific surface area to guide B-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) assembly, BGQDs aggregation could be effectively inhibited, and accelerated charge transfer and diffusion and improved the stability can be expected.

The recently developed Step-scheme (S-scheme) photocatalysts have attracted widespread attention attribute to enhanced charges separation and superior redox capacity [24]. Wang et al. synthesized S-scheme SnFe2O4/ZnFe2O4 heterojunction with non-toxic, strong magnetic and high catalytic activity by solvothermal method, and tetracycline removal reached 93.2% within 120 min [25]. Wang et al. synthesized S-scheme sulfur-doped g-C3N4 (SCN)/TiO2 heterojunction for efficient degradation of congo red [26]. To our knowledge, using efficient S-scheme heterojunction for antibiotics-heavy metals removals is still limited, and research on this regard should be desired.

In this work, Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr was synthesized by solvothermal method for simultaneous elimination of NFZ and Cr (VI). Physical-chemical properties and photoelectrochemical properties were comprehensive characterized. Meanwhile, effects of different systems and operational condictions on photocatalytic activity were also determined. More importantly, the mechanism of Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr for removal NFZ-Cr (VI) was discussed. In addition, possible removal pathways of NFZ and toxicity assessment of intermediates were also studied.

The nitrofurazone (NFZ), potassium dichromate(K2Cr2O7), indium (III) chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O) and silver nitrate (AgNO3) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Database. Potassium bromide (KBr), thioacetamide (CH3CSNH2, TAA),4-Vinylphenylboronic acid (C8H9BO2), boric acid (H3BO3), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and isopropanol (IPA) were obtained from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd.

The Zn3In2S6-BGQDs were synthesized by solvothermal method. Firstly, BGQDs solution was prepared. Then, 4-vinylphenylboronic acid (0.05 g) was dissolved in IPA (30 mL), denoting as A. Boric acid (0.10 g) was dissolved in ethanol (5 mL) solution, denoting as B. After mixing A and B, ultrasonic treatment was performed for 30 min, and the solution was denoted as C. Afterwards, H2O2 (5.0 mL) was added into solution C and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. Then, the solution was added to Teflon autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 12 h. The final solution was filtered by polytetrafluoroethylene microporous membrane (0.22 μm) to collect the BGQDs solution. Secondly, 2.0 mmol of InCl3·4H2O, 3.0 mmol of ZnSO4·7H2O, appropriate CH3CSNH2 and 20 mL of BGQDs solution were added to deionized water (70 mL) and then subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. Afterwards, the solution was transferred to Teflon autoclave and heated at 160 ◦C for 12 h. Then, obtained sediment was filtered and collected, and the yellow precipitate was washed for three times. The sample was dried at 80 ℃ for 24 h. The as-acquired Zn3In2S6-BGQDs photocatalyst was denoted as ZB. For comparison, Zn3In2S6 was prepared under the same conditions without addition of BGQDs solution.

Preparation procedure of Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr heterojunction was as follows: Certain weight of the Zn3In2S6-BGQDs (0.986 g) powder was added into AgBr precursor solution with ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. Then, the mixed solution was subjected to hydrothermal reaction at 160 °C for 6 h in a Teflon autoclave. Finally, Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr was washed for several times. The Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr sample was dried at 80 ◦C, denoting as ZBA. For comparison, AgBr-BGQDs was obtained without addition of the Zn3In2S6 powder, denoting as AB. Zn3In2S6/AgBr was obtained without addition of the BGQDs, denoting as ZA. The synthesis process was shown in Fig. S1.

The microstructures of materials were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-SU8020), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out on a diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku D/MAX 2500v). The chemical compositions and electron binding energies were examined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250XI + ). Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL, ZLX-PL-I) spectra of the samples were obtained at excitation wavelength of 325 nm. The time resolved photoluminescence (TRPL, Edinburgh FLS1000) were conducted to obtain fluorescence lifetimes of photogenerated carriers. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were examined on the UV2600 spectrophotometer. Besides, the work functions of AgBr (111) and Zn3In2S6 (102) planes were calculated using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP). ESR spectra were recorded to evaluate radical signals by Bruker-A300 paramagnetic resonance spectrometer. Besides, the transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott-Schottky (M−S) plots of various photocatalysts were tested on the electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, CHI-660 E). The three-electrode system was consisted of a photocatalysts working electrode, a Pt wire counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode, with Na2SO4 solution (0.50 M) as electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operations for photocatalytic removals of pollutants and analytical methods
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performances were evaluated in a water-cooled glass reactor by suspending 0.5 g/L of as-prepared photocatalysts in simulated wastewater containing NFZ (10.0 mg/L) and Cr (VI) (10.0 mg/L). Before photocatalytic reaction, dark adsorption was conducted for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium of mixed solution and photocatalysts. After that, visible light was irradiated to the reactor from the top using a 300 W Xe lamp (λ > 420 nm, light intensity of ∼ 375 mW/cm2). The concentrations of pollutants were obtained by a UV–vis diffuse reflector (UV-2600) at wavelengths of 375 and 540 nm for NFZ and Cr (VI). In addition, degradation intermediates of NFZ were measured by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS, AGILENT 6460; C18 column, 1.7 μm, 2.1 mm × 50 mm). The mobile phase was acetonitrile and pure water (volume ratio is 80: 20), and the flow rate was 0.3 mL/min. To clarify dominated reactive species contributing to NFZ and Cr (VI) removals, 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and KBrO3 were used as scavengers for ∙O2 –, ∙OH, h+ and e-, respectively.

The morphology and microstructure of AgBr, Zn3In2S6, ZB, ZBA materials were presented by SEM. Fig. 1 a shows that the AgBr exhibited spherical structure composed of aggregated particles of 0.5–5 μm with rough surface and plenty of pores (Fig. 1b). Zn3In2S6 presented a microsphere-like flowers structure, composing of petal-like nanosheets with smooth surfaces (Fig. 1c-d). The overall morphology of ZB was similar to that of the Zn3In2S6 (Fig. 1e-f). However, BGQDs cannot be observed, which might be due to its low content [23]. Comparatively, AgBr in the ZBA composite was almost completely coated by the ZB (Fig. 1g-h). It is noteworthy that the surface of the ZBA composite became rough and the aggregates were tightly bound to the ZB surface. Interestingly, the petal-like structure on the surface of ZBA was closer than those of ZB and Zn3In2S6, indicating the close contact between AgBr and ZB.

The TEM image of ZBA in Fig. 1i also showed that the AgBr was wrapped by microsphere-like ZB. Meanwhile, some BGQDs with average size of 3.52 nm were evenly distributed on Zn3In2S6 surface without agglomeration (Fig. 1j). As can be seen from Fig. 1k, the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm conformed to (1120) plane of the graphite structure, indicating successful synthesis of BGQDs in the ZBA [22]. In addition, the lattice fringes with the d-spacing of 0.32 and 0.33 nm were corresponded to (102) crystal plane of hexagonal phase Zn3In2S6 andthe (111) plane of AgBr [16,27].

XRD patterns of the AgBr, Zn3In2S6, ZB, ZBA samples were revealed in Fig. 2 . The AgBr could be indexed to a cubic phase of AgBr (JCPDS No. 06–0438) [28]. The peaks at 2θ values of 26.5◦, 31.0◦, 44.2◦, 55.0◦, 64.4◦, and 73.2◦ were in good accordance with (111), (200), (220), (222), (400), and (420) diffraction crystal planes, respectively. Both Zn3In2S6 and ZB shared the same crystal planes of the (005), (102), (110) and (203) at 21.8◦, 28.1◦, 47.5◦ and 56.2◦, respectively, corresponding to hexagonal phase (JCPDS No. 80–0835) of Zn3In2S6 [29]. This indicated that the BGQDs modification did not change the crystal structure and phase of Zn3In2S6. Besides, for ZBA samples, XRD pattern showed diffraction peaks of both AgBr and Zn3In2S6. In ZBA composites, the diffraction peak intensity of AgBr was weak, which may be owing to its low content. Overall, XRD results demonstrated that the ZBA heterojunction with good crystalline structures were successfully obtained.

The surface areas and pore size distribution of ZBA, ZB, Zn3In2S6 and AgBr were analyzed by N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. S2a, the isotherms of ZBA, ZB and Zn3In2S6 were all type IV isotherms with H2 hysteresis loops. The isotherms of AgBr were type II isotherms. The pore size distribution of the samples was mesoporous structure, and ZBA showed the largest specific surface area (86.25 m2 g−1), higher than Zn3In2S6 (79.62 m2 g−1) and AgBr (1.131 m2 g−1) (Table S1).

XPS results were displayed in Fig. 3 . The survey spectra of the ZBA (Fig. 3a) indicated that Ag, Br, Zn, In, S, B, C and O elements were observed, and B, C and O signals belonged to BGQBs. Fig. 3b-i present that the peaks of Ag 3d at 367.5 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.7 eV (Ag 3d3/2) and Br 3d at 68.7 eV (Br 3d5/2) and 69.8 (Br 3d3/2) could be indexed to Ag+ and Br− of AgBr, respectively [30,31]. Moreover, peaks at 1022.0 eV and 1045.1 eV in Zn 2p of Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 was assigned to Zn2+. Meanwhile, In 3d showed two different peaks at 444.7 and 452.3 eV, attributing to In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively. The S 2p peaks assigning to S2 could be divided into two peaks at 161.6 eV (S 2p2/3) and 162.8 eV (S 2p1/2), respectively [32,33]. Furthermore, two prominent peaks of B 1 s at 190.3 and 191.2 eV were assigned as the BC2O and BCO2 bonding, verifying that BGQDs contained rich oxygen-rich functional groups, which could facilitate promoting photocatalytic activity [34]. In addition, the XPS calculation results showed that atomic ratio of B was 4.31%, indicating that B was successfully doped into the carbon lattice of GQDs. In detail, the peak of C 1 s was centered at 284.6 eV, and the deconvoluted spectra included peaks at 284.0, 285.4, 286.6, and 288.0 eV, respectively, which were assigned to the chemical bonding of C-B, C-O, C = O and O-C = O, respectively [22]. Moreover, the O 1 s peaks at 531.0 and 532.4 eV were attributable to C = O quinone groups and C-O-C ether groups. The results suggested that the ZBA sample was successfully prepared.

UV–Vis DRS spectra in Fig. 4 a displayed light absorption ability of the samples. With modification of BGQDs, absorption peaks of the ZB composite showed slight red shift compared to that of the Zn3In2S6, which indicate BGQDs could broaden the visible light absorption range of composite materials.Furthermore, the visible light absorption capacity of the ZBA was further enhanced owing to the compositing with the AgBr.Typically, the energy band-gap values of the AgBr, ZB and ZBA can be valued using the Kubelka-Munk function [35]. The Eg values of the AgBr, ZB and ZBA photocatalysts were about 2.45, 2.70 and 2.38 eV, respectively, corresponding well with the Eg plots (Fig. 4a).

In addition, the energy band positions of AgBr and ZB were determined by Mott-Schottky curves (Fig. 4c-d). The positive slopes indicated AgBr and ZB were typical n-type semiconductors [29]. The flat band potential of AgBr was about −0.05 eV vs Ag/AgCl, and its CB and VB positions were 0.05 eV (vs NHE) and 2.4 eV (vs NHE), respectively. Likewise, the conduction band (CB) of the ZB was −1.0 eV (vs NHE) while its valence band (VB) potential was calculated to be 1.7 eV (vs NHE).

As revealed in Fig. 5 a, the steady-state PL intensity of ZBA was lowest, indicating that its carriers separation was the most efficient [36]. TRPL spectra was further used to characterize the lifetime of photogenerated charges (Fig. 5b). All the two-exponential fitting results indicated that photogenerated carriers of the ZBA exhibited the longest lifetime (τ = 5.260 ns), while the ZA presented a shorter lifetime (τ = 4.824 ns), followed by AB (τ = 4.725 ns), AgBr (4.515), ZB (4.175) and Zn3In2S6 (τ = 3.367 ns).The results showed that BGQDs could improve lifetime of photogenerated charge. Meanwhile, the extended lifetime of photogenerated electrons were also attributed to the “S-scheme” mechanism, which could be beneficial to improving the photocatalytic activity.

As revealed in Fig. 5c, the ZBA showed significant higher photocurrent response than those of ZA, ZB, AB, Z and AgBr, likely owing to the heterojunction structure of the ZBA composite.This result was in accordance with PL data and TRPL result.EIS results showed that the ZBA exhibited the smaller charge transfer resistance [37], because BGQDs modification could accelerate electron transport and improve the conductivity of the material.All above results confirmed that the ZBA heterojunction possessed superior charges separation and photocatalytic performance.

As depicted in Fig. 6 a-b and Fig. S3, very low removals of NFZ and Cr (VI) were observed from the blank group and dark adsorption group (10.50% and 12.00% adsorption percentage of NFZ and Cr (VI)). The ZBA photocatalysts displayed excellent photocatalytic activity for removals of NFZ (95.23%) and Cr (VI) (99.39%) after 2 h; followed by the ZA photocatalyst with NFZ degradation of 85.06% and Cr (VI) removal of 88.83%. The ZB and Zn3In2S6 displayed similar photocatalytic performance to that of the ZA. The AgBr and AB showed relatively poor photocatalytic activity. The satisfactory performance of the ZBA heterojunction could be ascribed to its superior light response ability, more efficient separation of carriers, and faster transport of charges on the interface. The Fig. 6c-d shows the degradation of NFZ and Cr (VI) reduction by photocatalysts accorded with pseudo-first-order kinetics model (Table S2). Compared with some reported photocatalysts (as listed in Table S3), the Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr displayed comparable or even better photocatalytic performance for antibiotics/Cr(VI) removal, showing that it was a competitive and promising material for combine pollutants removals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various factors on photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Fig. 7 a, in the NFZ-Cr (VI) coexistence system, simultaneous elimination of NFZ and reduction of Cr (VI) by the ZBA outperformed the cases of individual pollutant removal. The removal of NFZ and Cr (VI) by the ZBA reached 86.14% and 95.01% within 60 min; while, in the single reaction system, the removal of NFZ was 74.74%. This implied that there was a synergistic photocatalytic effect between NFZ degradation and Cr (VI) reduction.

The photocatalytic efficiency of the ZBA photocatalyst in NFZ-Cr (VI) complex polluted system was studied by fixing the initial Cr (VI) concentration (10 mg/L) and the amount of photocatalyst (0.5 g/L), and changing the co-existing NFZ concentration (5, 10 and 15 mg/L) (Fig. 7b). On the whole, the highest pollutants removals were attained under the circumstances with the initial concentration of 10 mg/L for both NFZ and Cr (VI).In addition, Cr (VI) reduction occurred more readily as compared with NFZ degradation.

Additionally, the effect of ZBA photocatalyst dosage on NFZ photooxidation and Cr (VI) photoreduction were also studied. As depicted in Fig. 7c-d, when the dosage of photocatalyst was increased from 0.3 to 0.5 g/L, the removals of NFZ and Cr (VI) were significantly improved, which was likely due to the increase of active species generated from the photocatalyst surface with the increase of photocatalytic dosage. However, further improved the dosage to 0.6 g/L, almost negligible improvements were observed as compared with that of 0.5 g/L. Possible reason could be that the opacity of the solution and light scattering can affect the photocatalytic activity [8]. In summary, the optimal catalyst dosage was 0.5 g/L.

Fig. 8 a-b displayed the influence of initial pH on eliminations of NFZ and Cr (VI). The Fig. S4 showed the degradation of NFZ and Cr (VI) reduction was in accordance with removal kinetics model under different initial pH (Table S4). The pH of the control experiment without solution pH adjustment was about 7. As shown, weak acidic aqueous condition (pH = 5) was more conducive to removals of NFZ and Cr (VI). The removal efficiency of NFZ decreased sharply when the initial pH value was higher than 5 (within the range of 7–11); also, when the initial pH value was less than 5, the removal decreased slightly. Furthermore, Cr (VI) photoreduction were decreased with the increase of pH value. Obviously, the formation of Cr (OH)3 under alkaline conditions would shield the reaction sites of the photocatalyst, resulting in decline of the photoreduction performance [38]. The reduction of Cr (VI) followed the equation:
(1) Cr2O7 2- + 14H+ + 6e- → 2Cr3+ + 7H2O
(2) HCr2O7 - + 7H+ + 3e- → Cr3+ + 4H2O
(3) CrO4 2- + 4H2O + 3e- → Cr (OH)3 + 5OH-

The photocatalytic activity of the ZBA was further assessed in different water bodies. As revealed in Fig. 8c-d, generally, the photocatalytic performance followed the order of: deionized water > lake water > tap water. In detail, the removal rates of NFZ in deionized water, lake water and tap water were 95.23%, 79.81% and 43.04%, respectively. In addition, the Cr (VI) photoreduction rates were 99.39%, 79.36% and 29.39%. Some inorganic ions such as Na+, Ca2+ in tap water could negatively affect the photocatalytic reaction because they could consume radicals [39]. However, coexisting inorganic ions and organic matters in lake water can balance the enhancement of organic matters and the limitation of inorganic ions [40].

Fig. 8 e-f reveal the influence of inorganic ions on NFZ degradation and Cr (VI) reduction. As shown, NaSO4, NaCl and NaNO3 exhibited negligible influence on photocatalytic activity of the ZBA. However, Na2CO3 greatly affected the photocatalytic activity, with NFZ removal decreased from 95.23% to 38.78%, and Cr (VI) elimination from 99.39% to 15.61%, as compared to the control. This may be because that the addition of Na2CO3 increased the pH value of the solution. This was coincided with results in Fig. 8a-b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the ZAB in NFZ-Cr (VI) coexistence environment
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of photocatalysts was determined by running five consecutive cycles for pollutants removals. Meanwhile, the chemical stability of the recycled ZBA was also characterized by XRD. Fig. 9 a shows that after five cycles, excellent photocatalytic activity toward NFZ-Cr (VI) removals from the ZBA could be maintained. Furthermore, XRD patterns of the tested ZBA were similar to that of the fresh material (Fig. 9b), indicating that the ZBA had good chemical stability.The SEM and TEM images of ZBA showed that the morphology of ZBA hardly changed after reaction, demonstrating that ZBA had good structural stability (Fig. S5). In addition, the leaching of elements during the reaction was analyzed by ICP-OES/MS to evaluate the stability of the materials (Table S5). The results showed that the average leaching rates of Ag, In and Br for the five cycles were 0.53%, 0.75% and 0.56%, respectively.

The main active species and photocatalytic mechanism of the ZBA photocatalyst for NFZ and Cr (VI) removals were revealed in Fig. 10 . Obviously, the addition of IPA showed strong inhibition on NFZ removal, with removal decreasing from 95.23%% to 42.53%, demonstrating that the ∙OH radicals played a crucial role in NFZ degradation.Furthermore, h+ and∙O2 – were also important active species for NFZ degradation. Therefore, the influencing degree of active species for NFZ degradation followed the order: ∙OH > h+ > ∙O2 –. Meanwhile, the presence of KBrO3 significantly retarded Cr (VI) reduction, indicating that the e- was the most responsible for Cr (VI) reduction. Furthermore, the Cr (VI) reduction rate slightly decreased from 99.39% to 76.33% in the presence of BQ, indicating that the ∙O2 – active species played a role in Cr (VI) removal.

ESR measurements was performed to further confirm the generation of ∙O2 – and ∙OH from the illuminated ZBA composite. As shown in Fig. 10b-c, no characteristic peaks in dark, while the signals belonged to DMPO-∙O2 – and DMPO-∙OH were clearly observed under visible light irradiation.

To further illustrate the mechanism, the work functions of the AgBr (111) and Zn3In2S6 (102) planes were calculated by DFT simulation according to the HRTEM result. As can be seen from Fig. 11 a-b, the work functions of AgBr and Zn3In2S6 were 7.00 eV and 6.12 eV, respectively. Under the circumstances, when the AgBr catalyst was closely contacted with the Zn3In2S6 in darkness, the electrons spontaneously migrated from the Zn3In2S6 to the AgBr across their interface until their Fermi levels reached equilibrium (Fig. 11c-d).In the process of electron migration through the ZBA interface, the interface region near Zn3In2S6 became positively charged due to electron loss, resulting in the upward bent of band edge of the Zn3In2S6. Contrarily, electrons migration to AgBr led to the accumulation of electrons and downward bent of band edge of the AgBr; herein, an internal electric field was created at the interface between the AgBr and Zn3In2S6. Besides, compared with the AgBr and ZB, the M−S slopes of ZBA increased, illustrating the thickness of their depletion layer increased (Fig. S6) [41]. This also indicated the formation of an internal self-built electric field after the AgBr and Zn3In2S6 was combined to construct a heterojunction. Under light irradiation, both AgBr and Zn3In2S6 were transformed into excited states by exciting electrons from their VB into CB. Due to the internal electric field and band edge bending, some of the electrons of the AgBr were easy to recombine with the holes of the Zn3In2S6, leaving electrons of the Zn3In2S6 and holes of the AgBr to participate in redox reactions (Fig. 11e).

Accordingly, the photocatalytic mechanism of the S-scheme ZBA heterojunction could be proposed (Scheme 1 ). For AgBr and AB, typical signal peaks of DMPO-∙OH were appeared because the holes in VB of AgBr (2.40 eV) could provide sufficient driving force to oxidize adsorbed surface hydroxyls to produce ∙OH (E (∙OH/ OH–) = 1.99 eV vs. NHE)) (Eq. (5)) [16]. Meanwhile, the h+ and ∙OH of ZBA were consumed to react with NFZ directly. Given the fact that the VB potential of ZB and Zn3In2S6 (1.70 eV) was more negative than the ∙OH/OH– potential (E (∙OH/ OH–) = 1.99 eV vs. NHE)), the illuminated ZB and Zn3In2S6 sample cannot generate ∙OH.Moreover, ZB and Zn3In2S6 had more negative CB (-1.0 eV) edge than O2/∙O2 − (-0.33 eV vs NHE), hence, the electrons generated on the CB of ZB could reduce O2 to ∙O2 − (Eq. (6)) [15]. Therefore, ∙O2 − radicals will also contribute to the degradation of NFZ. Overall, S-scheme composite could be formed between AgBr and ZB.Therefore, the electrons of AgBr recombined with holes of ZB, thus reserving electrons (CB of ZB) and holes (VB of AgBr) with strong redox capacity for NFZ and Cr (VI) removals. More importantly, NFZ could adsorb h+, which led to the synergistic effect between elimination NFZ and Cr (VI), further inhibiting the recombination of e− and h+ (Eq. (7–8)). The reaction steps can be described as follows:
(4) ZBA + hv → h+ + e−
(5) h++ H2O→ ∙OH
(6) O2 + e-→ ∙O2 −
(7) Cr (VI) + e- (ZB)/∙O2 − →Cr(III)
(8) NFZ + h+ (AgBr)/∙OH /∙O2 −→ products → CO2 + H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathways of NFZ and toxicity analysis of the intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: Possible degradation pathways of NFZ according to LC-MS analysis were proposed.The mass spectra of the identified intermediates of NFZ degradation were shown in Fig. S7, and major identified intermediates were listed in Table S6.The furan ring-containing identified intermediates and two linear chain products had prominent protonated molecular ion [3].Specifically, P1 could be converted to intermediate P2 through the nitro group (–NO2) of NFZ being reduced to hydroxyamino group (HOHN–), as well as P5 to P7. Then, the intermediate P3 and P8 could be produced from P2 and P7 through the nitro group (HOHN–) of NFZ being degraded to amino group (NH2–). And then, C = N unsaturated bond reduction, N-N bond and C-N unsaturated bond being attacked [3]. Furthermore, P9 was broken into two linear chain products.Finally, the NFZ could be mineralized to CO2 and H2O through redox.

The toxicity could be divided into extremely toxic, toxic, harmful and non-hazardous [44]. As shown in Fig. 14 , LC50/EC50/ChV values of most intermediates increased, indicating that toxicity of NFZ was significantly reduced after degradation. Overall, the intermediates were more toxic to daphnia and algae than to fish. In the aspect of acute toxicity, only P8 was more toxic to daphnia than NFZ. In terms of chronic toxicity, P2, P3, P4 and P8 were relatively harmful. Meaningfully, many intermediates were even harmless. In general, the ecotoxicity of NFZ decreased after ZBA degradation.

In this work, the (Zn3In2S6-BGQDs/AgBr) composite was synthesized by solvothermal method with Zn3In2S6 and BGQDs as precursors.The ZBA composite showed desired morphology and excellent carriers separation efficiency. Results of ESR, free radical trapping experiments and calculated work function together confirmed the carriers transfer pathway of ZBA followed S-scheme heterojunction mechanism. The photocatalytic performance the ZBA for simultaneous removals of NFZ and Cr (VI), with removal efficiency of 95.23% for NFZ and 99.39% for Cr(VI) within 2 h of visible light irradiation.More importantly, in NFZ-Cr (VI) coexistence system, the removal of pollutants showed synergistic effect.Besides, operational conditions including initial NFZ-Cr (VI) concentration,photocatalyst dosage, initial pH, water source, and inorganic anions etc. exhibited some effects on photocatalytic performance for pollutants elimination.Moreover, the NFZ degradation pathways were proposed, and the ecotoxicity of intermediates was reduced.This work advances the development of environmentally friendly catalyst for concurrent removal of refractory pollutants and heavy metals in aqueous environment.

Jiangli Sun: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jinhang Yang: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation. Jiaxiang Liang: Visualization, Validation. Lingli Tu: Data curation, Validation. Yuejing Bin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yanping Hou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707021), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2017GXNSFBA198186, 2020GXNSFAA297034), Foundation of Research Project of Bosch Branch of Guangxi University Graduate School (GXU-BFY-2020-036), and Central Government Funds for Guiding Local Scientific and Technological Development (2021ZYZX2089).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2022.121563.

ce:section.ce:para:
